The creek changed color for about two days. Muddy brown, like something upstream churned up the bottom
Tracks that size do not come from anything I recognize. Too wide for a wolf, too narrow for a bear
The ridge shifted after the last heavy rain. Not much, but enough that the old trail does not line up with the pass anymore
Fish stopped appearing in the lower pool. The upper stretch still has them, so something is blocking the run between the two
A fox den appeared under the granary foundation. The vixen moved her kits in overnight and the farmers are divided on whether to chase them out or leave them be
Bats left the eastern caves all at once. A whole colony, gone overnight. They usually roost there year-round
There is a patch of ground near the old foundation where nothing grows. Bare soil in a circle, maybe ten paces across
The hawks are nesting lower than usual this season. The falconer says that means the prey has moved down from the heights
A fallen tree blocked the lower trail for a week before anyone cleared it. The wood underneath had gone soft and white, like it rotted in days instead of months
The rock face on the north side is weeping moisture where it never did before. Something underground must have shifted
A landslide blocked the ravine after the tremor. Small one, but enough to dam the stream and create a new pool
The wildflowers bloomed a full month early. Everything is out of sequence and the pollinators have not caught up
An old growth stump near the ruins is sprouting new shoots. Whatever killed the original must have finally leached out of the soil
The owls moved out of the bell tower after nesting there for years. Something drove them off, but the tower looks the same as always
The lichen on the bridge pilings has turned a deep rust color. The stoneworker says it means the water chemistry changed, but she cannot say why
The moss on the standing stones grows on the wrong side. It should face the moisture but it faces the ridge instead
There is a sinkhole forming in the meadow south of here. Slow, but the edges are crumbling inward a little more each week

The soil near the old forge site has a metalite discoloration that extends about twenty paces in every direction. Nothing planted there tastes right. The herbalist says the groundwater carries the taint
A pair of herons moved into the upper pond this season. They have never nested here before, which the birdwatcher says means the fish population has reached a threshold worth their attention
The water table has risen noticeably in the last two years. Cellars that were dry for a decade are now seeping. Something changed upstream and nobody can say what
The oak blight spread from the western stand to the trees along the road. The forester says it follows the prevailing wind. If that is true, the orchard is next
An entire hillside of ferns turned brown in the space of a week. Not seasonal. Something in the soil shifted. The root systems were intact but the fronds looked scorched
The predators that used to den nearby have not been seen in two seasons. Either they moved on or something displaced them. I would like to know which
A spring that had been dry for years started flowing again after the tremor. Clean water, good volume. The engineers are debating whether to build an intake or leave it alone
The birch trees along the creek are showing stress bands in the bark. Horizontal cracks, evenly spaced. The pattern is more regular than weather alone would produce
A beaver dam appeared on the lower creek without anyone noticing the beavers. Well-built, so they have been here for weeks at minimum. The farmers downstream are not happy
The fungal growth on the fallen logs in the eastern wood has changed color from white to a vivid orange. The herbalist collected samples and says she has not seen the species before
Deer are coming closer to the settlement than they used to. The sentries see them at the tree line every morning. Either they are less afraid or they are being pushed from somewhere else
The root system of the old cedar has cracked the watchtower's foundation stones. The mason says the tree has to come down. The elders say the tree was here first. The foundation continues to crack
A colony of ground-nesting wasps established itself on the footpath to the lower well. Nobody wants to disturb the nest because the last person who tried was stung forty times
The river carved a new channel during the spring flood and left the old crossing point on dry land. The ford stones are sitting in a field now, twenty paces from the nearest water
A stand of young pines died simultaneously despite being healthy the previous season. The forester says the soil pH changed, likely from runoff. He blames the tannery. The tanner says prove it
The migratory birds arrived three weeks late this year. Half the usual numbers. The birdwatcher has kept records for fifteen years and says this is the lowest count on record
A thermal vent in the limestone outcrop keeps a small area warm enough for plants to grow year-round. Ferns, mosses, even flowering species in the dead of winter. The herbalist guards it jealously
The geologist who passed through said the river used to flow twenty paces east of where it runs now

A massive spiderweb complex appeared in the abandoned barn overnight. Not one web: dozens, interconnected, spanning the entire interior. The silk is unusually thick and the spiders themselves are larger than anything local
The groundwater near the mine entrance has turned acidic. Plants within fifty paces are yellowing at the edges. The miners say it is natural drainage. The farmers say it started with the mining
A tree struck by lightning last season is still standing but the bark peeled off in a perfect spiral from crown to root. The exposed wood is bone white and has not weathered
Fish cannot breathe in it and the downstream creek smells of rot. The herbalist says it will clear when the temperature drops, but the damage to the fish stock is already done
An underground stream surfaced in the basement of the old mill. Steady flow, about a bucket per minute, ice cold. The miller sees it as a problem. The brewmaster sees it as an opportunity
The songbirds disappeared from the eastern wood for a week. Then they came back, all at once, as if nothing had happened. The silence was more disturbing than any storm
The interior crystals catch the morning light and throw colored reflections across the rock face. It is stunning, and I give it a month before someone pries it loose
The elk rut started two weeks early this year. The bulls are aggressive and territorial well outside their normal range. The sentries have had to reroute two patrols to avoid confrontations

A patch of bioluminescent fungus appeared in the root cellar of the tavern. Faint blue-green glow, visible only in total darkness. The tavern owner sealed the cellar. The herbalist wants access. The negotiation is ongoing

The river otters have been fishing in the millpond, which was never part of their range before. Their presence means the natural pools are depleted. The fishermen confirm this: catches are down by a third
Blue-green, possibly copper ore. The smith is interested but the cliff face is unstable and nobody wants to mine it until the loose stone is cleared
The grape vines on the southern slope are producing fruit a full size larger than last year. The vintner credits the unusually wet spring. Soil has not changed, so the moisture must be the variable
Roughly circular, maybe thirty paces across. The water inside it looks the same as everywhere else but nothing lives in it.
The pine resin production has dropped sharply this season. The trees are not damaged, they are just not producing. The forester thinks it is a stress response but cannot identify the stressor
The mound is waist-high and the colony extends underground in every direction. The exterminator says he has never seen one this large and is reluctant to disturb it

The soil in the cleared field has a layer of charcoal about a handspan below the surface. Continuous, uniform, covering the entire field. Something burned there a long time ago: thoroughly, evenly, and completely
A vine species nobody can identify has been growing on the old stone wall. Aggressive, fast-growing, finding every crack in the mortar. The mason wants it removed. The herbalist wants to study it
The salmon run was the largest anyone can remember. So dense with fish you could nearly walk across on their backs. The bears have been seen closer to the settlement than is comfortable
The depression follows a straight line for about a hundred paces, as if something beneath the surface collapsed along a corridor

Dragonflies appeared in numbers three times normal this year. Swarms over the pond every evening. The mosquito population dropped to nearly zero within a week. Nature's pest control is more efficient than ours
The hillside terracing from the first settlers is eroding. The retaining walls were dry-stacked stone and the soil is washing through the gaps. Rebuilding would take a full crew for a season
A tree fell in the upper wood and the impact exposed a burrow system extending in multiple directions. Not a single den: a network. Whatever lives in there has been building for years
The river stones downstream of the hot spring are coated in a mineral deposit that forms in thin, colorful layers. Red, yellow, white, in bands. The layers grow about a finger's width per year
The clay deposit on the south bank has a vein of pure white kaolin running through it. She is being territorial about it, which I understand
Larger than a hawk, darker coloring, and a call that sounds like tearing fabric. The farmers just want it to keep eating the rodents
The aquifer feeding the main well is recharged by snowmelt from the upper ridge. In a low-snow year, the well drops by midsummer. The snowpack was below average and signs are showing
She says they fruit in the year following a burn, something about the ash chemistry triggering the mycelium. She mapped every burn site and harvests them on a rotation
It moves about a handspan per season. Slow, but when it reaches the bridge pilings it will be a problem
The stack is getting impressive. The mason says the weight will eventually damage the fascia
The forest floor is bare except for fungi and shade-tolerant mosses. It feels like walking through a building with a living roof
The formations are delicate and growing: thin curtains of stone, mineral pools with rims. The water is drinkable and faintly sweet
The meadow grass in the lower field grows taller and greener in a stripe about three paces wide that runs the entire length of the field.
The drumming is constant and audible from the road. I would not walk under it
A stand of aspens on the hillside share a single root system. When one tree is stressed, the others show symptoms. Technically one organism. The root mass could be centuries old
The farmers say it targets different insects. The fireflies, either way, are gone
A limestone cave near the upper trail has formations that drip a mineral-rich water. Geological patience
It now covers several acres of former pasture and the thorns are long enough to puncture boot leather. Removing it costs more than planting it did, which is the usual story with invasives
The water carries dissolved minerals that stain the rocks orange where it emerges. The discoloration has been spreading, which means the flow rate has increased
They whistle when anyone approaches, which the sentries appreciate as an inadvertent early warning system. The quartermaster is less grateful because they have been into the root vegetables
The treeline has moved downhill by at least a hundred paces in the last decade. Seedlings have established where there used to be open meadow.
Sea creatures, here, hundreds of miles from any coast. The geologist says this was all underwater once.
The wild boar population has exploded since the wolves left. Without predators, the boar root up the forest floor and destroy the understory. The balance the wolves maintained is gone
The animals know the hunters know about it. Neither side has adjusted strategy
The ancient oaks along the ridge have a canopy so wide that nothing grows beneath them for twenty paces in every direction. The ground is a carpet of dry leaves and acorns.
The ford is deeper now and the bottom is unstable. The crossing is still usable but not safely
The lichens on the standing stones grow about a finger's width per year. If the rate has been constant, the oldest colonies suggest the stones have been standing for at least four hundred years
A new hot spring appeared in the lower valley after the last tremor. Small, barely warm, but the mineral content leaves a white crust on every surface the water touches
The bridge keeper says the weight is not a concern. The structural engineer who visited said otherwise
Cutting the surface growth does nothing. You have to excavate the entire root network or it regenerates within a season

The spring peepers started calling two weeks before the last frost. Their timing is usually reliable enough to plant by. This year they were wrong and farmers who trusted the frogs lost their seedlings
The arch is maybe eight feet tall and four feet wide. It was not
The birdwatcher says it will rebalance within two seasons. The farmers say the rodent damage cannot wait two seasons
The mat of vegetation floats on water and the whole thing undulates like a living floor. It is disorienting and the footing is treacherous if you do not know the solid paths
A mineral vein in the cliff face oxidizes in bands: rust red, malachite green, sulfur yellow. After rain, the runoff paints the rock below in streaks of color that last until the next dry spell
The roots follow fracture lines and widen them over decades. The trees are literally breaking the mountain apart, one crack at a time
The hedgehog population has collapsed in the lower fields. The farmers blame the hedgehogs for digging in the beds.
A colony of burrowing owls has moved into the old quarry. The quarry has not been this productive in years, though not in the way anyone intended
Nobody minds. It means the system is healthy
Whistle into one and the sound resonates. Whistle into the next and it falls flat
The crayfish in the upper creek have grown to an unusual size. Claws as long as a finger. The population is small but the individuals are enormous, suggesting they have outlived their competitors
In the off years, the energy goes into root expansion. The pattern is so reliable that the children know which years to bring baskets and which years to stay home
In total darkness it is invisible. In dim light, it marks the path
A web of packed-earth channels, some extending hundreds of paces. An entire winter's architecture, exposed for a week, then overgrown
The bark on the windward side is smooth and polished. The bark on the lee side is rough and thick.
The creek pools below the waterfall harbor a population of fish that grow no larger than a finger. The pool is isolated: too high for upstream migration, too steep for downstream.

The soil near the old charcoal site supports only one grass species: coarse, blue-green, tolerant of residual alkalinity. Everything else dies. It has formed a perfect circle mapping the original burn area
The nest is now large enough to be visible from the settlement. The fishermen consider them good luck
The ash is pale grey, fine-grained, and compresses like powder. Whatever produced it was far away but powerful
Standing in the middle, you can see both: one side in full flower, the other still budding. The bees work the early side first and the late side keeps them fed longer
The branch is worn smooth where it perches. The fishermen noticed the disruption before they noticed the broken branch
The dead wood provides structure. The system built itself
Food preservation depends on reliable cold. The cold is no longer reliable
Where a tree fell and opened the canopy, the ferns fill the space exactly. Nature wastes nothing
In another decade it will be a meadow. The willows will have created the conditions for their own displacement
The herbalist says the population spike is cyclical: every seven or eight years, they overwhelm their food source and then crash. The caterpillars do not
The sandbar at the river bend has shifted position three times in two years. Each flood deposits sediment differently. The ferry operator adjusts the landing point after every high water event
A pair of ravens has nested on the watchtower for the last four years. They are particular about their people
It grows only on wood that has been submerged in running water for a specific period. The bridge provides the exact conditions by accident
The root mass is taller than a person and the soil clinging to it contains layers of history: charcoal fragments, pottery shards, and a seam of gravel from a stream that no longer exists
The birds return to within inches of the same spot each year. The falconer says the site has likely been in continuous use for centuries
The prints are detailed enough to show skin texture. The geologist measured them at nearly two feet across
The herbalist harvested what she could and the rest fell and fermented on the ground. The wasps discovered the fermented fruit and the fence line became impassable for a week
A natural spring in the rock face flows through a crack barely wider than a finger. The water pressure is enough to project a stream two paces from the cliff.

The moth migration passed through the settlement at night and the morning revealed thousands of them resting on every vertical surface. Wings folded, perfectly still, covering the walls in a layer of brown and grey.
The layer is only accessible at low water and only in a section about fifty paces long. She guards the location as if it were a vein of gold
At dawn the light catches them and they glow. The forester says the whiteness indicates a specific mineral content in the soil that the birches concentrate in the bark
The creek produces a natural sound that changes pitch with the water level. At high water it is a low roar.
Each groove is a miniature ecosystem: ferns, mosses, and small plants growing in the protected channels while the flat surfaces are barren and wind-scoured
The birdwatcher attributes it to the decline in the sparrowhawk that used to prey on them. Balance, when disrupted, produces surprises
An erosion gully that started as a minor wash three years ago has widened to six feet and deepened to four. The farmer says it will reach his fence by next season
The ducks approve. The fishermen do not

Their arrival date is consistent to within three days each year. The departure date has been getting earlier, which the birdwatcher says indicates a shift in the insect emergence pattern that the swifts depend on
The fallen oak in the clearing has become a nurse log: seedlings growing from its decomposing trunk in a straight line. The dead tree is more productive than it was alive, in its way
A seam of iron-rich stone in the creek bed stains the water orange for about two hundred paces downstream. Visitors think the creek is polluted.
The badgers have excavated a network that the forester estimates extends thirty paces in every direction. The soil they have moved over the decades is measured in tons
The herbalist found three plants there that she had only seen in dried specimens from distant climates. The meadow is sheltered from every wind by the surrounding canopy
The carter refuses to drive after dark during that period. The toads do not care about the inconvenience they cause
Fungi decompose the heartwood. The living trees around it host half as many species.
The outside bank erodes while the inside bank deposits. The surveyor says the river has moved twelve paces in a decade.
The elderflower season lasted only ten days this year instead of the usual three weeks. She harvested what she could but the syrup production is a third of normal
Topsoil, clay, gravel, sand, and a dark band of organic material that the forester says was a forest floor, buried by centuries of accumulation
By morning the riverbank was carpeted with their bodies. The fish fed for a week on the surplus
The farmer expected the worst but the foxes have been hunting rabbits in the field and ignoring the chickens entirely. The farmer does not trust the arrangement and the foxes do not care
The clay soil in the western field cracks in a hexagonal pattern during dry spells. Each hexagon is roughly the same size.
They consume a row of seedlings overnight. One absence cascades
A hollow oak near the crossroads has been adopted by a swarm of bees. The honey they produce has a distinctive flavor that the herbalist says comes from the oak tannins in the heartwood.
The creek water is clearest in early spring before the algae grow and clearest again in late autumn after the first frost kills them. The rest of the year, you are guessing
Mosses at the base, ferns in the crevices, ivy on the face, and a small tree growing from the capstone. The wall is more alive than built
The ring is now over thirty paces across. The forester says the mycelium network beneath it is a single organism and one of the largest living things in the area
Each hole is precisely the same diameter. The colony returns every year and adds more
The wetter the spring, the stronger the garlic. This year the smell reached the settlement
Good for ink. Bad for oaks
The silt is mineral-rich and the crops that season were the best in a decade. The flood was a disaster that produced a bounty
The crickets have gone silent in the western field while the eastern field is as loud as ever. The farmer denies using anything unusual

A woodpecker has been storing acorns in the gatehouse timber. Hundreds of them, pressed into every crevice. The gatekeeper considers it damage. The birdwatcher considers it architecture. The acorns are sprouting

The river otter below the bridge has become so accustomed to people that it fishes in plain view. The fishermen consider it competition. The children consider it entertainment. The otter considers them irrelevant
A heron within a month. The pond is less than a year old and already has a complete food web
The herbalist wants them studied. The thistles want nothing and continue to spread
You can run your fingers across them and feel individual ridges. The slab is a museum that nobody curated
Whether treatment will work is unknown. The disease has a perfect record elsewhere
They hatch, grow, breed, and die in the six weeks the pool persists. When the pool dries, their eggs wait in the dust until the next spring
Visitors are startled. Residents barely notice anymore
The foresters could not save it and the lumber is contaminated with beetle galleries. Even in death, it belongs to the beetles
Each pool is deeper and colder than the one above it. The children swim there every summer regardless
The dormice in the hedgerow are so numerous this year that you can hear them rustling from the road. Next year, when the hazels rest, the dormice will crash
The fishermen say this is impossible. The eel says otherwise
The tendrils have worked into the mortar joints and are now structural: the ivy holds the wall together as much as the wall supports the ivy. Removing it would collapse the corner
The pattern is as detailed as any drawing and was produced in an instant. The tree survived
It is taller than a person and the nearest matching granite is fifty miles north. The stone traveled here under ice and has been sitting in the field ever since
The pond has developed a thermocline in summer: warm water on top, cold on the bottom, with a sharp transition. The fish stay in the cold layer and the swimmers stay in the warm one
The storks are considered good luck and the council is not in a position to test that belief

The nettle patch behind the storehouse grows taller than a person by midsummer. Nettles indicate nitrogen-rich soil: something organic decomposed there in quantity. Nobody knows what was buried, but the nettles are grateful
It must have come from far upstream, traveling for miles in the high water. The wood is dense, dark, and harder than any local timber
The color is visible from the settlement. Travelers who arrive during the bloom say it is the most striking natural feature they have seen
The roots bear the weight of the masonry and the masonry shelters the roots from weather. Neither planned it, but the partnership works
The ecosystem near {subject_focus_the} has shifted in ways the naturalist says would normally take years
The soil composition near {subject_focus_the} has changed. Higher mineral content, different pH
The plant die-off near {subject_focus_the} follows a clear boundary. Inside it, nothing. Outside, normal growth
Water samples from near {subject_focus_the} show trace elements that are not present upstream
The insect population near {subject_focus_the} has exploded. Species that are usually rare are now dominant
Fungal growth near {subject_focus_the} is aggressive. It has colonized surfaces that normally resist it
The root systems near {subject_focus_the} are growing toward the surface instead of downward. Unusual behavior
A wildlife census near {subject_focus_the} showed a complete absence of small mammals. None. Not one
The algae in the water near {subject_focus_the} is a species the herbalist has not encountered before
The tree bark near {subject_focus_the} shows stress marks consistent with rapid environmental change
Seeds that blew in from near {subject_focus_the} germinated in the settlement garden. The resulting plants are oddly vigorous
The bird population near {subject_focus_the} has changed composition. Predatory species have moved in where songbirds used to be
The groundwater near {subject_focus_the} has a sulfurous quality that was not present last season
Animal trails near {subject_focus_the} all divert around a central area. Something is being avoided
The lichen patterns near {subject_focus_the} have reversed their usual growth direction
Pollination near {subject_focus_the} has declined sharply. The fruit trees closest to it bore nothing this season
The worms near {subject_focus_the} have surfaced in dense clusters. The soil below may have become uninhabitable
A biologist examined the moss near {subject_focus_the} and said it is producing spores at an accelerated rate
The predator behavior near {subject_focus_the} has become erratic. Hunting at odd hours, ignoring usual prey
A patch of mutated clover was found growing near {subject_focus_the}. Extra leaves, abnormal coloring
The creek bed near {subject_focus_the} is lined with a mineral deposit that was not there two months ago
The oldest trees near {subject_focus_the} are shedding bark. Not diseased, according to the forester. Stressed
The migration pattern near {subject_focus_the} has shifted. Flocks that used to pass over now detour around it
Decomposition near {subject_focus_the} is either accelerated or halted depending on the material. No consistency
The natural balance near {subject_focus_the} is disrupted. Every indicator the naturalist tracks is outside normal range
